items.q: Which part of the psyche operates on the reality principle?
items.why: Students confuse ego (reality) with superego (morality). The ego mediates between id's demands and reality's constraints. The superego is the internalised moral conscience.

items.q: How does a securely attached infant respond to reunion with the caregiver?
items.correct: Seeks comfort and is easily soothed, then returns to play.
items.misconception: Shows no distress and ignores the caregiver.
items.random_wrong: Cries continuously and cannot be comforted.
items.why: Students confuse secure with avoidant. Secure infants DO show distress at separation and seek comfort at reunion - this contact-seeking is the marker of security, not indifference.

items.q: Why is high IQ a problem for the statistical infrequency definition?
items.correct: It is statistically rare but not abnormal/undesirable.
items.misconception: Because high IQ is actually common.
items.why: Students miss that statistical infrequency lacks a value judgement - it just measures rarity. High IQ is rare but desirable, exposing the definition's limitation.

items.q: Whose 1932 study used War of the Ghosts to demonstrate schema effects?
items.why: It's Bartlett. “Loftus” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Flashbulb memories were first studied by:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Brown and Kulik. “Loftus and Palmer” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year did Tversky and Kahneman publish their heuristics paper?
items.why: The real figure is 1974 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
